findings.summary: mcp_bridge.py imported 10 private (_-prefixed) symbols from mcp/ subpackage
evidence: TOOL_DEFINITIONS, RESOURCE_DEFINITIONS, PROMPT_DEFINITIONS were named with _ prefix but imported cross-module.
9 _tool_* functions in proxy_tools.py were imported into parent package mcp_bridge.py and re-exported in __all__, indicating they are public API not private implementation.
findings.suggestion: Remove underscore prefix from symbols that cross module boundaries to match their actual access pattern. Consider if mcp_bridge.__all__ exposing tool handlers is necessary or if callers should import from mcp.proxy_tools directly.

findings.summary: 18 import cycles detected in the codebase including dependency_injection and utils/process
evidence: Holistic scan reports 18 existing import cycles.
dependency_injection/container.py -> dependency_injection/scopes.py cycle and utils/process mutual cycle are documented examples.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared types/interfaces into separate modules that both sides can import without creating cycles. For dependency_injection, separate the registry from the scopes protocol.

findings.summary: 3 functions in canvas.py are pure passthroughs (80-100% arg forwarding) with no added logic
evidence: create_file_node (4/5 forwarded, 80%), create_link_node (5/5 forwarded, 100%), create_text_node (5/6 forwarded, 83%) detected as passthrough by desloppify.
These add no policy, translation, or validation on top of what they delegate to.
findings.suggestion: Either inline the underlying dataclass construction at call sites, or add meaningful validation/defaulting logic to justify the wrapper layer.

findings.summary: agents/cli/handlers.py has 34 top-level functions across 17 disconnected clusters (756 LOC)
evidence: Module-level mutable _droid_controller at line 656 modified from 1 site.
Mixed responsibilities: 34 top-level funcs across 17 disconnected clusters (14, 5, 1, 1, 1, +12 more).
Largest cluster has 14 functions.
Module docstring appears after imports (lines 38-44).
findings.suggestion: Split into focused modules: handle_agent_ops.py, handle_droid_ops.py, handle_stream_ops.py. Each cluster represents a coherent set of operations.

findings.summary: agents/cli/handlers.py (756 LOC, 1 importer) has been reopened as untested critical 2 times
evidence: desloppify reports 10,196 open test_coverage findings.
handlers.py (untested critical, 756 LOC, 1 importer, reopened 2 times).
Test health dimension sits at 8.6% of 144,701 checks.
Zero-mock policy is good (prohibits MagicMock/monkeypatch) but critical paths remain untested.
findings.suggestion: Prioritize coverage for high-importer, high-LOC modules. Create integration test fixtures that exercise CLI handlers end-to-end without mocking.

findings.summary: Overall test health at 8.6% indicates most files lack adequate test verification
evidence: 22,131 tests exist but test_health dimension reports 8.6% of 144,701 checks.
The zero-mock policy is the right strategic direction, but implementation coverage of critical modules lags significantly.
Many modules added in recent sprints have minimal test depth.
findings.suggestion: Add a pre-commit hook or CI gate that blocks new modules without accompanying test files. Target critical high-importer files first.

findings.summary: handlers.py module docstring appears after 25+ lines of imports, not at module top
evidence: Lines 1-25 are imports.
The module docstring 'CLI Handlers for Agents Module...' appears at lines 38-44 after import block.
This is a hallmark AI-generation pattern where the docstring is inserted as a comment block rather than as the proper module-level string.
findings.suggestion: Move module docstring to line 1 (before imports) to follow Python convention and make module purpose immediately visible.

findings.summary: handle_info catches 8 specific exception types where a broader except would be equally informative
evidence: handle_info() catches (AgentError, ClaudeError, CodexError, GeminiError, JulesError, OpenCodeError, ValueError, RuntimeError, AttributeError, OSError) — 9 distinct types.
This level of specificity provides no recovery behavior differentiation; all paths do the same thing (log + print_error + return False).
findings.suggestion: Consolidate to except Exception for top-level CLI handlers where recovery is impossible. Reserve specific exception types for cases where you take different actions per type.

findings.summary: 6 modules fail import due to missing optional SDK dependencies, silently reducing capability
evidence: MEMORY.md documents: '6 optional modules fail import (cloud AWS/Azure/GCP, performance.monitoring, lazy_loader, security cert validator)'.
These modules load as empty stubs, which can surprise callers expecting real functionality.
findings.suggestion: Add explicit capability detection at module load time with clear error messages listing the required SDK install command. Consider marking these in module __init__.py with CAPABILITY_AVAILABLE=False.

findings.summary: Some modules return dict with error key, others raise exceptions — inconsistent at MCP boundary
evidence: proxy_tools.py functions return dict results.
trust_gateway.py raises SecurityError.
mcp/server.py catches SecurityError and converts to error dict.
This means callers at MCP boundary need to handle both patterns.
findings.suggestion: Standardize MCP tool handlers to always return dicts (let server.py handle exception→dict conversion). Non-MCP code can raise. Document this boundary contract explicitly.

findings.summary: Some module names lack specificity: maintenance, integration — directory purpose unclear from name alone
evidence: 122 modules in src/codomyrmex/.
Modules named 'maintenance' and 'integration' have ambiguous scope — maintenance covers health checks AND task management, integration is vague about what it integrates.
A new engineer cannot determine content from directory name.
findings.suggestion: Rename to purpose-driven names: maintenance → health_monitoring or project_health, integration → external_adapters or third_party_bridge.

findings.summary: Sprint audit found 38 zero-mock policy violations that required explicit remediation
evidence: Sprint 8 (Feb 26) fixed 38 violations across 32 files: 8 test files had monkeypatch usage, 4 stub fallback classes, 6 stub functions.
The zero-mock policy is stated in CLAUDE.md but violations keep reappearing (requiring periodic remediation sprints).
findings.suggestion: Add ruff rule or pytest plugin that detects unittest.mock imports and fails CI. Make policy mechanical rather than relying on audit cycles.

findings.summary: Not all 122 modules have the RASP documentation pattern (README+AGENTS+SPEC+PAI.md)
evidence: Sprint 12 (Feb 27) audit fixed 21 PAI.md files.
Sprint 21 (Mar 2) added PAI.md to 7 more modules.
As of latest state, some modules still lack full RASP compliance.
The convention exists and is documented but enforcement is manual.
findings.suggestion: Add a CI check that verifies every module directory has README.md, AGENTS.md, SPEC.md, and PAI.md. Automate the lint to prevent drift.

findings.summary: 122 modules in flat src/codomyrmex/ makes subsystem ownership boundaries hard to trace
evidence: The documented layer hierarchy (Foundation→Core→Service→Application) is coherent on paper.
But 122 modules in one flat directory means a new engineer must read the architecture doc to understand grouping — the filesystem doesn't show it.
Modules like agents/claude, agents/codex, agents/gemini, agents/jules, agents/opencode are siblings of very different capability levels.
findings.suggestion: Group modules into top-level category directories matching the layer hierarchy: src/codomyrmex/foundation/, src/codomyrmex/core/, src/codomyrmex/service/, src/codomyrmex/apps/. This makes the architecture self-documenting from the filesystem.

findings.summary: PAI integration uses 3 parallel channels (MCP, direct Python, filesystem) with no clear guidance on when to use each
evidence: PAI.md documents three integration channels: MCP (primary), Direct Python, and Filesystem back-channel.
While MCP is marked primary, callers in the codebase use different channels without clear decision criteria.
api_handler.py imports tool_pai_status directly (bypassing MCP server dispatch).
findings.suggestion: Document the decision rule for when to use each channel in a single ADR. Enforce MCP as the only public channel by removing direct tool function re-exports from mcp_bridge.__all__.

findings.summary: data_visualization/advanced_plotter.py at 1,023 LOC exceeds maintainable threshold for a single module
evidence: MEMORY.md documents this file at 1,023 LOC as a known oversized file.
A single file of 1,023 LOC handling 'advanced plotting' spans multiple responsibilities (chart types, rendering, export, configuration).
The file is hard to navigate and test atomically.
findings.suggestion: Split by chart type: line_plotter.py, bar_plotter.py, scatter_plotter.py, heatmap_plotter.py + plotter_base.py for shared rendering. Each file should handle one chart family.
